dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a nosebleed and possible lacerations.
Hi, I'm Nick, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've had a constant nosebleed for about an hour now.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your name, the date today, and what brought you to call 911?
Yes, my name's Robert, the date is the 30th. I was walking to the bathroom, and my nose started bleeding.
Thank you, Mr. Smith. Your eyes are open, and you seem alert to your surroundings-how are you feeling right now?
I'm okay. It just keeps bleeding, and the towel is full.
It sounds like you understand clearly. Let's take some steps to help manage the bleeding.
Mr. Smith, squeeze my finger for me.
Okay.
Your airway is clear. I see you breathing fine, but I'm concerned the nosebleed might interfere. We should keep it open and clear during care.
We're going to insert gauze into your nose to help reduce the bleeding.
Gauze is in. Seems to be some pressure helping, but it's still bleeding a bit.
Mr. Smith, are you having any trouble breathing at all?
No, I can breathe just fine.
Breathing rate is 20, and equal in depth and effort. No signs of labor or cyanosis noted.
How do your hands and feet feel to you right now?
Warm and dry.
Strong and regular radial pulse, 98 beats per minute. Skin appears warm and dry, no pallor noted-no signs of shock at this time.
Can you tell me the medication you are currently taking?
Aricept, Flomax, and Glypizide.
Any history of nosebleeds or problems with your blood pressure?
I have osteoarthritis and high blood pressure, but no nosebleeds.
What did you last eat or drink?
Dinner was three hours ago.
Anything you'd like to add about the events leading up to the nosebleed?
I just started walking to bathroom, and it began right away. No trauma.
Any allergies to medications or known allergens?
No.
When did the nosebleed first start?
Right when I started walking to the bathroom about an hour ago.
Does the nosebleed feel like it's getting worse or any pain when trying to breathe?
It's not painful, but the blood is running.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your discomfort today?
Zero. I'm not in pain.
Pulse is 98 beats per minute and regular.
Respirations are 20 per minute; depth is normal. No abnormalities in quality or pattern noted so far.
Blood pressure is 208 over 118. Slightly elevated but stable at the moment with no signs of additional distress.
Oxygen saturation is 98%. Normal reading for you based on how you're breathing now.
I'll suction your nose briefly to check for any clots and ease pressure around that area slightly if possible during the handling process here today.
Could not clear out any clots but reinjected gauze carefully back into place. Let's watch this closely while we assist with transport operations today as well.

Mr. Smith, please try to lean forward a bit more to help control the flow of blood from your nostrils better than just trying to hold pressure alone manually all the time during care sessions now as well.

Gauze is still in place, bleeding is significantly reduced but not stopped. Let's monitor the trend closely moving forward given history provided already by both patient and girlfriend herself about AMA status already being taken prior to our arrival today.

We'll monitor you closely and keep pressure on your nose to see if it controls the bleeding better, especially once we get on board safely for transport later tonight as part of protocol action plan being set forth now already regarding this case here today too.
Skin is normal in color, warm, and dry. No signs of jaundice or changes in general appearance noted at this time either way from either side of team here involved currently after briefing.

Do you have any headaches, dizziness, or neck pain? We're looking around here too just in case there are other issues not yet discussed by Mr. Smith himself today when he arrived on scene here initially first thing tonight...

Nope, nothing like that. Just focus remained solely on handling nosebleed so far according to statements made earlier about what happened right before calling emergency services personnel were even contacted properly at point zero today...

Have you noticed any swelling in your nose or face? Let me visually confirm with my own eyes moving forward now as part standard operating procedure for this type incident handling required at all times regardless whether issue seems minor initially or not really...

Just bleeding. No visual signs of injury present currently meaning no obvious external damage visible yet today so far thus far from EMTs' perspective while examining him under proper lighting conditions here inside home environment at night as well.

Mr. Smith, I need to check your mental state. Are you aware of where you are and what's happening? Please answer everything as honestly as possible please considering your age (85 years old) might affect responses when under stress conditions experienced today during nosebleeding episode right here outside bathroom room door location...

Yes, I know I need to stay still and keep pressure on my nose. Still fully oriented to surroundings and able to process what is going on currently about us here today due mainly to ongoing communication happening between me and each member working on case here so far even in stressful situations involving myself mainly so far today upon arrival at residence itself...

Great. You are alert and oriented ×4-your mental status is intact, so that's reassuring. We can count on accurate reporting moving forward with further evaluations needed then in subsequent steps taken later in processes today as well...}

Glucose reading is not on scene-we will not perform one now. Your medications like Tamsulosin (Flomax), donepezil (Aricept), and Metformin (Glypizide) don't currently indicate a need to check this additional parameter at this point in time unless something pops up urgently during transport tonight.}
We will not perform an EKG as it's not required based on your current condition. However, we will monitor any cardiac symptoms or changes closely.}

We'll follow the hemorrhage control protocol and keep an eye on your vitals during transport tonight as well as any additional changes that may arise during next steps in care process ongoing right now.}
Mr. Smith, which hospital would you like us to transport you?}
[COMPANY_NAME], please.}
We'll assist you to a stretcher while you hold pressure on your nose.}
Mr. Smith is in position, covered, and secured with a 5-point restraint system.}
Pulse is 102 now, so it's elevated-watch it closely.}
Respiratory rate stays at 20.}
Your blood pressure is now 196 over 130.}
Oxygen is at 100%.}
For completeness, you also take donepezil and metformin.}
You're placed in the ambulance, secured for transport. Your girlfriend can ride with us if that's okay.
Yes, I'd like her to come.}
She'll be fine in the seat with a seatbelt harness.}
All data and treatment notes will be handed off to the hospital nurse en route.}
Five-point restraint double-checked for transport safety.}
Mr. Smith is alert, oriented ×4, and seems to understand what's going on.}
Gauze fell out at the ambulance, so we now use a towel to support pressure manually.}
Your initial symptoms included vomiting clots. Could you describe what led up to that incident?}
It was just once, and the blood was from my nose, I think.}
En route to [CITY], constant monitoring will take place.}
Once at the ED, we'll give the detailed situation and care plan to the nurse.}
